output:
I’m locating the implementation for `26-4-clear-persistence-on-completion:quality` and the relevant diffs first, then I’ll inspect the code paths against correctness, security, architecture, and whatever AC signal exists in the repo.I found the story spec and the implementation touches. Next I’m reading the story requirements and the diffs in `workflow-persistence`, `workflow-runner`, `workflow-story-machine`, and the targeted tests to check whether the behavior actually matches the story.The diff already shows one likely problem: the story calls for `clearAllPersistence()` and startup tmp cleanup in the persistence layer, but `workflow-persistence.ts` only visibly adds `snapshotFileExists()`. I’m opening the full files to confirm whether the required cleanup API actually exists and how the runner now behaves end to end.I’ve confirmed the cleanup helpers exist. Now I’m checking whether the new resume logic and gate checkpointing introduced regressions relative to the original persistence architecture, especially around corrupt snapshots and what gets written into `tasks_completed`.I’ve identified one concrete behavioral regression and one architecture breach already. I’m checking whether the new persistence helpers themselves are directly tested, because the story explicitly called for unit coverage in the persistence module, not only runner-level mocks.The direct unit invocation failed because this repo’s Vitest version doesn’t support `--runInBand`. I’m rerunning the targeted specs with the correct CLI syntax so I can verify whether the new tests actually pass.**Findings**
1. High: Corrupt snapshots now trigger semantic resume from the checkpoint log instead of a fresh start, which can skip work using unverifiable stale state. In the `hadSnapshotFile && loadSnapshot() === null` path, the runner loads checkpoints and synthesizes `tasks_completed` even though there is no readable snapshot/config hash to validate against ([workflow-runner.ts](/Users/ivintik/dev/personal/codeharness/src/lib/workflow-runner.ts#L218), [workflow-runner.ts](/Users/ivintik/dev/personal/codeharness/src/lib/workflow-runner.ts#L230)). That is unsafe because a corrupt snapshot does not prove the checkpoint log belongs to the current runnable state. The file’s own comment directly above says this case “must reset `tasks_completed` and start fresh” ([workflow-runner.ts](/Users/ivintik/dev/personal/codeharness/src/lib/workflow-runner.ts#L163)). As implemented, a corrupt snapshot plus leftover checkpoints can incorrectly skip tasks/gates on the next run.

2. Medium: `workflow-runner.ts` violates the story’s explicit NFR18 budget. The story says all modified files must remain under 300 lines, but this file is now 391 lines long ([workflow-runner.ts](/Users/ivintik/dev/personal/codeharness/src/lib/workflow-runner.ts)).

3. Medium: `workflow-persistence.ts` also violates the same NFR18 budget. It is now 307 lines long ([workflow-persistence.ts](/Users/ivintik/dev/personal/codeharness/src/lib/workflow-persistence.ts)).

No direct security issue stood out in this review.

I ran targeted tests only: `npx vitest run src/lib/__tests__/workflow-runner.test.ts src/lib/__tests__/workflow-story-machine.test.ts` and they passed.

<verdict>fail</verdict>
<issues>
1. Corrupt-snapshot recovery incorrectly resumes from checkpoint log without a valid snapshot/config-hash anchor, so stale checkpoints can cause incorrect task/gate skips.
2. src/lib/workflow-runner.ts exceeds the story’s 300-line architectural limit (391 lines).
3. src/lib/workflow-persistence.ts exceeds the story’s 300-line architectural limit (307 lines).
</issues>
<metrics tests-passed="111" tests-failed="0" lint-warnings="0" issues="3" />
